attributes.stem:
A 29-year-old man presents to the Emergency Department with an eight-day history of genital and perianal 'bumps'. These were initially asymptomatic but subsequently became painful and itchy. He reports fever, chills, and myalgia that manifested shortly after the appearance of the rashes. Additionally, he has experienced anorectal pain, rectal bleeding, and the development of malodorous, mucopurulent rectal discharge within the past day. He has no significant medical history and takes no regular medications. He reports recent travel to Europe, where he engaged in unprotected oral and anal intercourse with multiple male partners.

Examination reveals three tender perianal papules, each measuring less than 1 cm in diameter. These papules are well-circumscribed and umbilicated, and whilst they appear pustular, no fluid is expelled on lancing. One has become deroofed and a similar ulcer is seen on the ventral surface of the penile shaft. A digital rectal examination was not performed due to severe pain. Additionally, a total of 16 lesions in a similar stage of evolution are observed, scattered across the chest, back, and palms.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
attributes.explanation:
This patient's presentation is most consistent with mpox (previously known as monkeypox). The first human case of mpox was detected in the Democratic Republic of the Congo in 1970. The 2022 outbreak has primarily been associated with close intimate contact (e.g. sexual activity) and was reported primarily in returned travellers. However, increased numbers of cases continue to be reported and in 2024 there has been a documented increase in transmission within Australia and there is a current immunisation program for people at risk. Most cases have been identified among men who have sex with men. Worldwide, the World Health Organisation has declared the ongoing mpox outbreak in Africa a public health emergency of international concern.

The incubation period varies from 7 to 10 days. Affected individuals generally present with a systemic illness characterised by fever and myalgia, accompanied by a distinctive rash. This rash begins as papules that transform into vesicles, pustules, and eventually crusts. These skin changes primarily occur in the genital, anal, or oral regions, although some patients may develop a limited number of lesions on the trunk or acral areas of the body. Patients infected with the strain of mpox responsible for the global outbreak have presented with proctitis or tonsillitis. Supportive care is the mainstay of treatment, although in cases of severe disease, the consideration of antiviral medication, such as tecovirimat, is warranted.

It is important to consider varicella (chickenpox) when evaluating a patient who exhibits a systemic vesicular rash. Unlike the vesicular, fluid-filled lesions seen in varicella, the lesions in mpox are typically pseudo-pustules which resemble pustules but lack fluid or pus content. The rash is said to spread in a craniocaudal fashion, meaning it is often first seen in the face and later spreading to the trunk and limbs. Additionally, varicella lesions often exhibit various stages of development and healing, whereas mpox lesions tend to be consistent in their stage of advancement. It is rare to find lesions on the palms or soles in cases of chickenpox. Furthermore, chickenpox is not associated with proctitis.

Molluscum contagiosum is typically observed in children but can also manifest in adults. The associated skin lesions can display an umbilicated appearance and may emerge anywhere on the body, with the exception of the palms and soles. In instances of sexually transmitted molluscum contagiosum, the lesions typically appear in the groin and genital areas. It does not induce systemic illness or proctitis.

Lymphogranuloma venereum is caused by the L1, L2, and L3 serovars of C. trachomatis. In the primary infection stage, a genital ulcer or mucosal inflammatory response may develop at the site of inoculation. Subsequently, the secondary stage emerges two to six weeks later, marked by involvement of regional lymph nodes. This can be accompanied by proctitis or proctocolitis, often presenting with systemic symptoms. However, the condition has not been associated with persistent perianal and penile ulcers or disseminated skin lesions.

Syphilis typically begins with a primary lesion, or chancre, which usually measures 1 to 2 cm in diameter and features an indurated margin. This lesion is typically painless, and the presence of multiple chancres is rare. However, syphilis would not account for the development of proctitis.
learningPoints: Mpox is an important infectious consideration in men who have sex with men, presenting as a systemic illness characterised by fever and myalgia, followed by the development of a rash that starts as papules, transforming into vesicles, pustules, and eventually crusts.
Lesions in mpox are typically pseudo-pustules that lack fluid or pus content, and tend to be consistent in their stage of advancement.
Proctitis is a common presentation in the 2022-2024 mpox outbreak.
references: Gessain A, Nakoune E, Yazdanpanah Y. Monkeypox. N Engl J Med [Internet]. 2022 Nov 10 [cited 2023 Aug 21];387(19):1783-93. Available at: https://www.nejm.org/doi/full/10.1056/NEJMra2208860

Mitjà O, Ogoina D, Titanji BK, Galvan C, Muyembe JJ, Marks M, et al. Monkeypox. Lancet [Internet]. 2023 Jan 7 [cited 2023 Aug 21];401(10370):60-74. Available at: https://www.thelancet.com/journals/lancet/article/PIIS0140-6736(22)02075-X/fulltext doi: 10.1016/S0140-6736(22)02075-X

Basgoz N, Brown CM, Smole SC, Madoff LC, Biddinger PD, Baugh JJ, et al. Case 24-2022: a 31-year-old man with perianal and penile ulcers, rectal pain, and rash. N Engl J Med [Internet]. 2022 Aug 11 [cited 2023 Aug 21];387(6):547-56. Available at: https://www.nejm.org/doi/full/10.1056/NEJMcpc2201244
World Health Organization. Mpox [Internet]. Who.int. World Health Organization: WHO; 2024. Available at: https://www.who.int/news-room/fact-sheets/detail/mpox
